description: CASE-Bench case set. Each case is a business problem (problem + hard constraints only — no solution mechanisms hinted) paired with a curated set of known reference solutions, each tagged with its core mechanism and a scope note. Models are scored on producing FEASIBLE, high-impact, original solutions; a separate diagnostic measures how far their ideas diverge from the known reference playbook (graded 0-3, not a binary match). The reference set measures CONVERGENCE WITH CONVENTIONAL SOLUTIONS — it is explicitly NOT a training-contamination detector.
reference_protocol: References were pooled via structured LLM ideation and author review, written at a consistent core-mechanism granularity with mutually-exclusive scope notes. They aim to tile the realistic solution space so genuine novelty is non-trivial; they are a finite sample, not a complete frontier.

cases.title: Regional bookstore chain bleeding to online retail
cases.context: A privately held 22-store regional bookstore chain (~$60M revenue) operating on long-term main-street and mall leases across mid-sized US Midwest cities, with no meaningful e-commerce arm and a payment/retail model built on walk-in transactions.
cases.prompt: A privately held 22-store regional bookstore chain has watched foot traffic fall 35% over five years as customers migrate to online retailers and e-books. Revenue per square foot is now the lowest in the chain's history and operating margins are wafer-thin. The brand still commands deep local affection, but the in-store experience feels dated and transaction volumes keep sliding. Leadership's mandate is to keep the physical estate viable for at least another decade; outright store closures and a pure pivot to pure-play e-commerce are off the table. Capital for reinvestment is limited, the leases are long and cannot be broken cheaply, and any change must preserve the chain's identity as a bookseller. Same-store economics, not headcount cuts, are what leadership wants fixed.

reference_answers.idea: Rent out underused floor area as a paid co-working, study, or quiet-membership zone that monetizes square footage directly.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any idea that charges customers for time/access to the physical space itself (memberships, day passes, reservable desks/rooms) rather than for products; distinct from renting space to other businesses (r11) and from hosting events (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Add an in-store cafe or food-and-beverage counter to lengthen dwell time and capture higher-margin consumables.
reference_answers.scope_note: Adding a complementary consumable category (coffee, food, bar) operated by the store to raise per-visit spend and dwell; not third-party concessions and not events catering.

reference_answers.idea: Program ticketed author talks, book clubs, workshops, and classes to generate recurring reasons to visit.
reference_answers.scope_note: Recurring programmed happenings that draw people in on a schedule; includes paid or free events, clubs, signings, classes. Distinct from renting the room out passively (r11) and from selling memberships to the space (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Build deeply curated hyper-local sections (local authors, regional history, staff niches) that online retailers structurally cannot replicate.
reference_answers.scope_note: Differentiating the book assortment itself toward local/curated/niche selection as a moat against online breadth; about WHAT is on the shelf, not who recommends it (r12) or store-exclusive editions (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Sell a monthly book-box or replenishment subscription that converts one-off buyers into predictable recurring revenue.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any subscription/standing-order model that turns transactions into recurring billing; distinct from a points/frequency loyalty program (r7) which does not pre-commit revenue.

reference_answers.idea: Use the stores as same-day pickup, returns, ship-from-store, or locker hubs for online orders.
reference_answers.scope_note: Repurposing physical locations as logistics/fulfillment infrastructure (BOPIS, returns, last-mile, lockers) for the chain's or partners' online demand; about moving goods, not selling space or events.

reference_answers.idea: Launch a points or tiered-rewards program to raise visit frequency and lifetime value.
reference_answers.scope_note: Incentive schemes (points, tiers, perks) that reward repeat purchasing without locking in recurring payment; distinct from subscription (r5) and from CRM targeting (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Diversify the product mix into higher-margin non-book goods like gifts, stationery, games, puzzles, and vinyl.
reference_answers.scope_note: Broadening SKU range into adjacent retail categories to lift basket margin; about selling new physical product types, not assortment depth within books (r4) or exclusive editions (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Open a wholesale/bulk channel to schools, libraries, and corporations, including school book fairs and curated corporate orders.
reference_answers.scope_note: Selling to organizational buyers in volume as a distinct revenue channel; B2B/bulk relationships, not consumer-facing retail or subscriptions.

reference_answers.idea: Add used-book buyback and trade-in, creating a circular, higher-margin inventory stream that pulls people in to sell.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any used/secondhand/trade-in/consignment model for books; about circular inventory economics and the foot-traffic hook of selling-in, not new-product category expansion (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Sublease or partition off excess square footage to an unrelated long-term tenant to cut occupancy cost in place.
reference_answers.scope_note: Passively reducing real-estate cost by shedding/leasing surplus space to outside tenants without operating it; distinct from running a co-working space yourself (r1) or hosting curated retail partners (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Productize staff knowledge as a personal book-recommendation or concierge service customers seek out.
reference_answers.scope_note: Monetizing or leaning on staff curation/recommendation as the differentiator (personal shopping, shelf-by-staff, concierge); about people/advice, not the underlying assortment (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Offer signed copies, store-exclusive special editions, and limited print runs unavailable online.
reference_answers.scope_note: Scarcity/exclusivity goods (signed, numbered, exclusive editions, bundles) that online channels cannot supply; about exclusive product variants, not breadth of local selection (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Sell transactional services such as gift wrapping, print-on-demand/espresso-book printing, and ticketing.
reference_answers.scope_note: Per-transaction value-added services performed in store; distinct from renting space (r1) and from food (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Curate rotating pop-ups and micro-tenants (local makers, complementary retailers) to create a marketplace-within-store that drives cross-traffic.
reference_answers.scope_note: Inviting curated outside sellers/pop-ups whose presence drives complementary foot traffic and shared revenue; differs from passive subleasing (r11) by being merchandised and traffic-generating.

reference_answers.idea: Use customer data to run targeted, personalized campaigns that win back lapsed shoppers and prompt repeat visits.
reference_answers.scope_note: Data-driven targeting/personalization/marketing to reactivate or retain customers; about messaging and analytics, not the reward economics of loyalty (r7).

cases.title: Budget airline aircraft turnaround time too slow
cases.context: A single-fleet (narrowbody) low-cost carrier of ~80 aircraft flying point-to-point across secondary airports in Southeast Asia, with thin fares, slot constraints at some airports, and revenue earned only while aircraft are airborne.
cases.prompt: A single-fleet low-cost carrier flying point-to-point across secondary airports in Southeast Asia earns money only when its aircraft are airborne. Average gate turnaround, wheels-down to next wheels-up, sits at 42 minutes against a best-in-class benchmark of 25, and every minute added across the fleet costs millions of dollars a year while stealing the daily rotations the network is built around. The carrier cannot acquire additional aircraft or secure new gates within the planning year, and it must hold its single aircraft type and full regulatory and safety compliance. Fares already sit at the floor of the market, so the answer cannot lean on charging passengers more to fly. The board wants the gap to best-in-class closed using only the assets already on the ramp.

reference_answers.idea: Choreograph fueling, catering, baggage, and cleaning to run concurrently rather than in sequence.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any reordering of ground-handling tasks from serial to simultaneous via a gate-choreography/critical-path plan; about overlapping existing steps, not eliminating or speeding an individual step (r5, r8, r13).

reference_answers.idea: Board and deplane through both front and rear doors at once using airstairs or dual jet bridges.
reference_answers.scope_note: Using more than one door simultaneously for passenger flow; distinct from the order passengers board in (r3) and from removing assigned seats (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Sequence boarding by zone, back-to-front, or window-middle-aisle to cut aisle and stowage congestion.
reference_answers.scope_note: Optimizing the SEQUENCE in which passengers are called to board through whatever doors exist; not the number of doors (r2) or seat assignment policy (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Drop assigned seating so passengers take the first available seat and stop hunting for rows.
reference_answers.scope_note: Free/open/unassigned seating to remove the seat-finding bottleneck; about seat-allocation policy specifically, not boarding sequence (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Have cabin crew clean and reset during taxi and flight so the aircraft lands nearly turn-ready.
reference_answers.scope_note: Collapsing the dedicated ground-cleaning step by doing it onboard before arrival; about removing the cleaning task from the critical path, not parallelizing it on the ground (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Shift passengers toward carry-on (via fees or policy) so less goes in the hold and loading time drops.
reference_answers.scope_note: Demand-shaping that cuts the VOLUME of hold baggage to be loaded; about reducing the loading workload, not parallelizing it (r1) or managing overhead bins (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Standardize a single cabin and galley configuration so cleaning, loading, and provisioning are uniform and fast.
reference_answers.scope_note: Configuration standardization across the fleet to speed servicing; about removing variability/complexity from the aircraft itself, not the catering content (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Eliminate hot meals in favor of pre-loaded standardized carts or buy-on-board to shorten the catering step.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reducing or pre-staging the onboard provisioning workload itself; about the catering step's content/method, not configuration standardization (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Pre-position ground crew and equipment so a pit-stop team starts the instant the aircraft chocks.
reference_answers.scope_note: Labor/GSE readiness and staging so no time is lost mobilizing after arrival; about resource positioning, not task sequencing (r1) or incentives (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Set explicit turnaround-time targets and reward ground teams for hitting them, F1 pit-crew style.
reference_answers.scope_note: Performance management, targets, accountability, and bonuses to motivate faster turns; about behavior/culture, not the physical setup (r9) or tooling.

reference_answers.idea: Reshape gate and pre-board flow so passengers are queued and processed to board the moment the doors open.
reference_answers.scope_note: Off-aircraft passenger processing so boarding starts immediately with no upstream delay; about the gate-side passenger pipeline, not onboard boarding mechanics (r2, r3).

reference_answers.idea: Instrument turnarounds with sensors and a live dashboard to find the critical-path delay and manage exceptions.
reference_answers.scope_note: Data/IoT/visibility tooling to detect and intervene on the binding constraint in real time; about measurement and exception management, not a specific physical change.

reference_answers.idea: Speed refueling with single-point pressure refueling, hydrant systems, or fueling with passengers onboard where permitted.
reference_answers.scope_note: Making the fueling step itself faster via method/equipment/policy; about the fueling task specifically, not parallelizing it with others (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Defer or skip lavatory/water/full cabin servicing on short turns, doing it only at base.
reference_answers.scope_note: Removing optional servicing tasks from short-turn gates entirely; about omitting steps, not speeding or parallelizing them.

reference_answers.idea: Redesign rotations and gate assignments (block-time buffers, de-banking, minimal-taxi gates) to protect and recover turnaround time.
reference_answers.scope_note: Schedule/network/gate-allocation changes that reduce turnaround pressure or variability; about planning the operation, not the ground tasks themselves.

reference_answers.idea: Enforce bag-sizing and gate-check standard bags so onboard stowage stops jamming the aisle during boarding.
reference_answers.scope_note: Managing carry-on stowage at the door/aisle to keep boarding flowing; distinct from reducing total hold baggage (r6) and from boarding order (r3).

cases.title: Outpatient clinic losing revenue to patient no-shows
cases.context: A network of 12 outpatient specialty clinics in a US metropolitan market on fee-for-service reimbursement with a mixed Medicaid/commercial payer base, where each appointment slot is perishable capacity and unfilled slots are unrecoverable revenue.
cases.prompt: A network of 12 outpatient specialty clinics in a US metropolitan market runs on fee-for-service reimbursement, so every unfilled appointment slot is revenue that can never be recovered, and a patient who went untreated while genuinely sick people wait weeks for an opening. The no-show rate sits at 18% and has stuck there despite the clinics' existing reminder program. Missed appointments cluster among younger patients, first-time visits, and Monday-morning slots. The clinics cannot turn patients away or violate payer and consumer-protection rules, clinical staff time is fixed and scarce, and patient goodwill is fragile, so frustrating people who do show up is unacceptable. Leadership wants the gap between booked and attended slots closed without simply working the front desk harder, and the fix must hold up across a heterogeneous, partly Medicaid patient population.

reference_answers.idea: Send escalating text, call, and email reminders with one-tap confirm or cancel as the appointment nears.
reference_answers.scope_note: Improving the reminder system itself (more channels, escalation, easy confirm/cancel); about prompting recall, not requiring active opt-in to keep the slot (r12) or behavioral framing (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Keep a standby waitlist that auto-fills a canceled slot within minutes.
reference_answers.scope_note: Filling slots that open up by pulling from a ready waitlist/short-notice queue; about recovering known cancellations, not preventing no-shows or overbooking (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Require a refundable deposit, card-on-file, or modest no-show fee to create skin in the game.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any monetary stake tied to attendance (deposit, fee, card-on-file penalty); distinct from positive rewards for showing (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Offer a virtual visit as a fallback so a missed in-person slot converts to a billable telehealth appointment.
reference_answers.scope_note: Using remote/virtual care to salvage or substitute for an in-person slot; about modality switching, not access barriers (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Predictively overbook only the slots statistically most likely to no-show, balancing fill against wait risk.
reference_answers.scope_note: Deliberate double-booking guided by no-show probability; about overbooking strategy, not backfilling actual cancellations (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Let patients self-cancel and self-reschedule instantly online instead of phoning the front desk.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reducing friction for patients to move their own appointment, freeing slots earlier; about self-service rebooking mechanics, not reminders (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Use implementation-intention prompts, social-norm messaging, or patient-written reminders to strengthen psychological commitment.
reference_answers.scope_note: Behavioral-science framing to increase intention to attend (have patient write the slot, cite peer attendance norms); about persuasion content, not channel/escalation (r1) or money (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Compress the wait from booking to visit via same-week or open-access scheduling, since shorter horizons no-show less.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reducing the booking-to-visit interval as the lever (open-access, same-day carve-outs); about timing horizon, not expanded hours (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Tackle transportation, parking, and childcare obstacles via rideshare partnerships, transit vouchers, or on-site childcare.
reference_answers.scope_note: Removing logistical/physical barriers that prevent attendance; about getting the patient there, not the appointment time options (r14) or modality (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Reward patients who show up with loyalty perks, gift cards, or a lottery entry.
reference_answers.scope_note: Positive reinforcement for attendance; the mirror of financial penalties (r3) and distinct from it because it rewards rather than charges.

reference_answers.idea: Concentrate the most intensive interventions on the segments that drive no-shows (first-time, young, Monday-morning).
reference_answers.scope_note: Segmenting patients by no-show risk and allocating effort accordingly; about WHO gets extra attention, not WHICH intervention is used.

reference_answers.idea: Release a slot unless the patient actively confirms it in advance, converting silence into an opening.
reference_answers.scope_note: Making attendance opt-in so unconfirmed slots are reclaimed early; about a confirm-or-lose policy, distinct from reminders that merely notify (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Build commitment before the visit with first-timer welcome calls and pre-completed intake so patients feel invested.
reference_answers.scope_note: Onboarding/relationship-building before the appointment to raise show-rate, especially for new patients; about pre-visit investment, not reminders (r1) or continuity of care (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Add evening and weekend slots so appointments fit patients' work and life schedules.
reference_answers.scope_note: Changing WHEN appointments are available to match patient availability; about scheduling supply/timing, not shortening lead time (r8) or removing transport barriers (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Assign a consistent provider and care navigator so the patient relationship itself raises attendance.
reference_answers.scope_note: Using continuity of care and human navigation/relationship to improve show-rate; about ongoing relationship, not a one-time pre-visit onboarding (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Run group or shared medical appointments so a single no-show no longer wastes a whole slot.
reference_answers.scope_note: Restructuring the visit format to many-patients-per-block so attendance variance matters less; about appointment structure, not overbooking (r5).

cases.title: Coffee chain dead between 2pm and 5pm
cases.context: A 400-site UK coffee chain of small high-street and transit-adjacent counter-service units with card/contactless-dominant payments, lean fixed staffing shifts, and ~70% of revenue concentrated before 11am.
cases.prompt: A 400-site coffee chain across the UK, built on small high-street and transit-adjacent counter-service units, takes roughly 70% of its sales before 11am. Between 2pm and 5pm the stores sit nearly empty while staff are still paid, rent still accrues, and equipment runs idle. The brand is firmly typecast as a morning-coffee destination and has no afternoon identity to speak of. Whatever leadership does, it cannot cannibalize the high-margin morning rush or alienate the commuter regulars who are the core of the business. Per-site capital is tight, the footprints are small, and staffing shifts are already lean. The goal is to make the 2-to-5 window earn its keep.

reference_answers.idea: Run an afternoon happy-hour price promotion to pull price-sensitive demand into the trough.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lowering price specifically in the 2-5 window to stimulate volume; about discounting/promo pricing, not loyalty-point mechanics (r6) or bundles (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Introduce afternoon-oriented products (iced and cold drinks, teas, savoury snacks, desserts) for non-coffee occasions.
reference_answers.scope_note: Adding products tuned to afternoon demand occasions; about WHAT is sold, not who it's aimed at (r4) or how it's branded as a ritual (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Reposition stores as afternoon third-places for remote workers and students with reliable wifi and outlets.
reference_answers.scope_note: Making the venue a place to linger/work in the afternoon; about dwell-oriented positioning and amenities, not renting the room out (r11) or targeting a youth segment (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Target the after-school crowd of teens, students, and parents who are free in the early afternoon.
reference_answers.scope_note: Pursuing a distinct non-commuter demographic that is available 2-5; about WHO is targeted, not the product (r2) or messaging channel (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Use the slack hours to produce catering orders and prep next-morning bulk demand from the idle kitchen and staff.
reference_answers.scope_note: Repurposing idle equipment/labor for production/fulfillment (catering, B2B bulk, next-day prep, commissary); about making things in the trough, not selling the afternoon menu via delivery (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Award bonus loyalty points or rewards redeemable only during the 2-5 window.
reference_answers.scope_note: Loyalty/reward economics gated to the trough to pull existing members in; distinct from a flat discount (r1) and from messaging pushes (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Manufacture an afternoon ritual ('afternoon treat' daypart) with branded set bundles that give people a reason to come.
reference_answers.scope_note: Creating and branding a new consumption occasion plus bundling to anchor it; about demand-occasion framing/bundles, not the raw menu items (r2) or discount (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Drive afternoon delivery and group/office orders through aggregators to reach demand that won't walk in.
reference_answers.scope_note: Selling to consumers through delivery/off-premise channels in the trough; about distribution channel, not in-house production for catering (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Convert the space into a different concept in the afternoon/early evening, such as a wine, dessert, or co-branded bar.
reference_answers.scope_note: Operating a distinct format/brand/menu identity during the window (alcohol, dessert, partner concept); about a concept switch, not just adding afternoon products (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Sell a monthly prepaid coffee pass designed to nudge incremental off-peak visits.
reference_answers.scope_note: Subscription/prepaid commitment that pulls forward and adds visits; about recurring prepay economics, not point-based loyalty (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Rent the room out in the afternoon for meetings, tutoring, private bookings, or small events.
reference_answers.scope_note: Charging third parties for use of the physical space during the trough; about renting space, not repositioning it for walk-in dwell (r3) or hosting open events (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Give morning buyers a 'come back this afternoon' voucher tied to their morning purchase.
reference_answers.scope_note: Cross-daypart attach that converts a morning transaction into an afternoon return; voucher linked to a prior purchase, distinct from standalone loyalty (r6) or discount (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Flex labor down and dynamically staff the dead hours to cut the cost side rather than chase revenue.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reducing idle-hour cost via dynamic scheduling/shorter shifts; the only cost-side lever, distinct from all revenue-fill mechanisms.

reference_answers.idea: Sell take-home retail in the afternoon (packaged beans, bottled cold brew, grab-and-go for the evening commute).
reference_answers.scope_note: Productizing for off-premise/take-home consumption later; about packaged retail goods, not on-premise afternoon menu (r2) or delivery of made drinks (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Host afternoon clubs, classes, language meetups, kids' story hours, or live acoustic sessions to pull traffic.
reference_answers.scope_note: Programmed community events/activities that generate footfall in the trough; about events drawing people, not renting the room (r11) or work-friendly amenities (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Use app, geofenced, or CRM nudges timed to 2pm to reach nearby or lapsed customers with afternoon offers.
reference_answers.scope_note: Data-driven, time-triggered marketing/targeting to pull traffic; about messaging and audience targeting, not the underlying reward (r6) or price (r1).

cases.title: Rural region with almost no EV charging adoption
cases.context: A rural shire government in regional Australia, covering long distances between small towns with a weak and expensive-to-upgrade distribution grid in many areas, operating under a constrained public capital budget and with no control over vehicle or equipment market prices.
cases.prompt: A rural shire government wants electric-vehicle use to take hold among both its residents and the travellers passing through, but uptake has stalled in a self-reinforcing deadlock: too few vehicles make charging sites look uneconomic, and the scarcity of charging makes the vehicles impractical. The shire covers long distances between small towns, has a constrained public budget, and in several areas the local electricity network is weak and costly to reinforce. Many residents are wary of a technology they associate with distant cities and have little firsthand exposure to it. Vehicle and equipment costs are set by outside markets the shire cannot influence, and any plan must work within existing grid limits and a small capital allocation. Leadership wants durable, self-sustaining adoption rather than a handful of showcase installations that sit unused. Propose distinct approaches to break the deadlock and make adoption stick.

reference_answers.idea: Site chargers where people already stop and dwell, such as diners, supermarkets, and motels, so the vehicle charges while the driver does something else.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any idea whose lever is WHERE chargers go, placing them at existing high-dwell destinations to piggyback on stops people already make. Excludes corridor fast-charging (r5) and home charging (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Electrify the shire's own and large local fleets (council vehicles, buses, delivery, agribusiness) first to guarantee steady charger utilisation.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is creating guaranteed baseline DEMAND via a captive/anchor fleet to make sites bankable. Counts fleet conversion, depot anchoring, employer fleets. Distinct from targeting individual sticky user segments (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Cover or underwrite the upfront charger cost for site owners through grants, tax credits, or revenue guarantees so they install despite low early traffic.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is reducing the financial RISK/COST to a third-party host of installing hardware (subsidy, grant, guarantee, rebate). Distinct from community co-ownership (r14) and from the charger funding itself via grid revenue (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Pair chargers with on-site solar and battery (or other local generation) so they sidestep weak or absent grid connections.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is bypassing grid limits with local generation plus storage. Counts any self-powered/islanded charging hardware. Distinct from selling grid services (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Fund high-power chargers along the main through-route to capture long-distance travellers passing through the shire.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever serves TRANSIENT through-traffic with fast charging on highways/corridors. Distinct from destination siting for locals (r1) and from regional network coordination (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Run ride-and-drive demos, loaner programs, and hands-on education so skeptical residents experience the technology before committing.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism reduces PERCEIVED risk/unfamiliarity through direct firsthand exposure or information. Counts demos, test drives, workshops, info campaigns. Distinct from using trusted local messengers as the channel (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Recruit respected locals, farmer co-ops, and existing dealerships as ambassadors who vouch for EVs to a community that distrusts outsiders.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is the CHANNEL of social proof: who delivers the message, leveraging existing trusted local relationships. Distinct from the content/exposure of education itself (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Pitch chargers to retailers as a footfall and dwell-time generator, so charging is justified by the extra in-store spend it brings rather than by charging fees.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is a BUSINESS-MODEL where the charger pays off via a complementary revenue stream (retail spend, dwell), not electricity margin. Distinct from capex subsidy (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Promote overnight at-home and on-farm charging, exploiting that rural homes have driveways and sheds, to cut dependence on the sparse public network.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever SHIFTS demand off public chargers to private overnight charging. Counts home chargers, farm/depot charging, granny-charging. Distinct from mobile/on-demand supply (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Provide flexible supply through charging trucks, battery swap, or on-demand top-ups that need no fixed permanent site.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism delivers charging WITHOUT fixed infrastructure: mobile units, swap, portable batteries, on-call top-up. Distinct from any fixed-site approach.

reference_answers.idea: Ensure every charger is usable by anyone through a single payment app and network roaming, so sparse chargers aren't locked behind incompatible memberships.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever removes ACCESS/PAYMENT friction so existing chargers are fully usable (interoperability, single app, no membership silos, open access). Distinct from adding more chargers.

reference_answers.idea: Grow the vehicle base directly via subsidised used EVs, group-buy schemes, or leasing so cost stops blocking residents from adopting.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism subsidises or de-risks the VEHICLE purchase (demand side), e.g., used-EV programs, bulk buys, leasing, loans. Distinct from charger-side subsidies (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Fund chargers by having them and connected vehicle batteries earn revenue providing demand response, storage, or vehicle-to-grid services to the network.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is monetising the charger/battery as a GRID ASSET (V2G, demand response, frequency/storage revenue). Distinct from off-grid generation that merely avoids the grid (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Let residents collectively own and finance chargers through a community cooperative, echoing the rural electricity co-op model they already trust.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is a community/cooperative OWNERSHIP-and-financing structure. Distinct from a host receiving a subsidy (r3) and from private business models (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Concentrate early effort on a high-mileage segment likely to adopt first, such as rideshare, delivery drivers, or government commuters, to seed dense usage.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is SEGMENTATION of demand: target a narrow high-utilisation early-adopter group to concentrate usage. Distinct from a captive anchor fleet you control (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Coordinate siting with neighbouring shires and regional planners so chargers across jurisdictions form one continuous usable network.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is inter-jurisdictional COORDINATION/planning to achieve network coverage no single shire could. Distinct from one shire's corridor chargers (r5).

cases.title: Mid-size art museum can't attract under-35 visitors
cases.context: A publicly subsidised regional art museum in a mid-size central-European city, operating on a tight budget with a board protective of its scholarly reputation, holding a respected permanent collection but seen by younger residents as formal and unwelcoming.
cases.prompt: A publicly subsidised regional art museum is watching its membership age and shrink. Visitors under 35 are uncommon, and those who do come almost never return for a second visit. The institution holds a respected permanent collection but carries a reputation among younger residents as formal and intimidating. Its operating budget is tight and its board is protective of the museum's scholarly standing, ruling out anything that would cheapen the institution. The objective is durable, repeat engagement from younger audiences, not a single viral moment or a one-off spike in attendance. Whatever is done must survive on modest resources and preserve the museum's curatorial credibility. Propose distinct approaches to win this audience and keep it coming back.

reference_answers.idea: Run after-hours evenings with music, drinks, and atmosphere so a museum visit becomes a social night out rather than a solitary cultural duty.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever reframes the visit as a SOCIAL/leisure occasion (lates, parties, ambience). Distinct from cultural-crossover content (r13) and hospitality amenities (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Lower the price barrier for young people through cheap entry, pay-what-you-want nights, or a low-cost membership tier.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is reducing the PRICE/monetary barrier to entry for the segment. Counts free nights, discounts, cheap tiers. Distinct from belonging-based membership (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Offer hands-on studios and workshops where visitors make or respond to art rather than passively view it.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever makes engagement ACTIVE/participatory (making, creating, interactive workshops). Distinct from interpretive aids that explain art (r6) and volunteer roles (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Partner with universities, student bodies, and local employers to reach young people through institutions they already belong to.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is a distribution CHANNEL/partnership to access the segment in bulk. Distinct from co-locating with adjacent venues for foot traffic (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Mount a frequent cadence of fresh, rotating, contemporary exhibitions so there is always a new reason to return.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever creates repeat visits through PROGRAMMING NOVELTY/turnover (always something new, FOMO). Distinct from a fixed recurring ritual cadence (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Replace scholarly labels with approachable storytelling, plain-language guides, and digital interpretation that make the art unintimidating.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism lowers the INTELLECTUAL/intimidation barrier by making the collection legible. Counts accessible labels, tours, AR/audio guides, storytelling. Distinct from participatory making (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Build a young-patrons circle with insider access, behind-the-scenes events, and identity, so members feel they belong to the institution.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is IDENTITY/belonging community building (membership as in-group, ambassadors, insider access). Distinct from cheap-tier pricing (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Stage photogenic, share-worthy moments and installations that prompt social posting and earned media.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism drives AMPLIFICATION via shareability/earned media. Counts Instagrammable installations, viral hooks. Distinct from between-visit digital relationship (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Add a good cafe, bar, or comfortable lounge so the building works as a place to linger, meet, and return to.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever upgrades on-site AMENITY/comfort to extend dwell and give a reason to come. Distinct from social-event programming (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Take art out to where young people already are through pop-ups, off-site activations, and presence at city events.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism MEETS the audience off-premises rather than waiting for them. Distinct from partnering with their institutions (r4) and co-locating with neighbours (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Capture young visitors' contact details on the first visit and use personalised follow-ups to prompt a planned second visit.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is the RETENTION PLUMBING: data capture plus personalised return-prompts/recommendations. Distinct from any specific programming; this is the nudge machinery.

reference_answers.idea: Establish a fixed habitual touchpoint such as a monthly members' club night or subscription that turns visiting into a routine.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism builds HABIT through a regular fixed cadence/subscription. Distinct from novelty rotation (r5), which relies on change rather than rhythm.

reference_answers.idea: Bridge the collection to young-relevant culture by tying exhibitions to music, film, fashion, or gaming.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever makes content RELEVANT by linking art to adjacent youth culture. Distinct from social-occasion atmosphere (r1) and from demystifying existing works (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Bring young people inside as docents, volunteers, interns, or advisory contributors so involvement deepens loyalty.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism deepens engagement by giving the audience a ROLE/labour stake in the institution. Distinct from passive participatory art-making (r3) and from affinity membership (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Co-locate or time programming with adjacent draws like festivals, nightlife districts, or neighbouring venues to capture passing crowds.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever borrows EXISTING foot traffic via co-location/timing with nearby attractions. Distinct from outreach that goes off-site (r10) and institutional partnerships (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Sustain a relationship between visits through an online community, podcasts, or social content that keeps the museum present in young people's lives.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism maintains engagement DIGITALLY between physical visits. Distinct from one-off shareable spectacle (r8) and from CRM transactional nudges (r11).

cases.title: Supermarket throwing away perishables at scale
cases.context: A regional supermarket chain in the United Kingdom operating many stores of varying size and turnover served by regional distribution centres, under a publicly announced corporate sustainability commitment, where shoppers expect full, fresh-looking shelves until closing.
cases.prompt: A regional UK supermarket chain discards roughly 8% of its fresh produce, bakery, and prepared foods before it can be sold. The loss hits margins directly and works against a public sustainability commitment the chain has already announced. Shoppers expect shelves and counters to look full and fresh right up to closing time, which pushes stores toward generous stocking of short-life items. The chain runs many stores of very different size and turnover, served by regional distribution centres, and cannot afford changes that visibly degrade product availability or the brand's quality image. Any approach must hold up across stores with sharply different sales patterns. Propose distinct approaches to cut the waste without undermining sales or brand perception.

reference_answers.idea: Cut prices on items approaching their sell-by date to move them before they expire.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever clears at-risk stock by lowering PRICE (markdowns, reductions, yellow-sticker, time-based discounting). Distinct from non-price demand steering (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Donate unsold edible surplus to food banks and charities through a reliable collection arrangement.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism redirects edible surplus via DONATION to charity/redistribution networks. Distinct from paid secondary resale (r3) and inedible valorisation (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Sell surplus through discounted surprise-bag apps or a surplus B2B marketplace as a separate clearance channel.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever moves surplus through an ALTERNATE paid sales channel (surprise-bag apps, surplus marketplaces, outlet). Distinct from in-store markdowns (r1) and free donation (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Transform unsold produce and bakery into prepared meals, soups, and juices that earn value-added margin.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism REPROCESSES near-waste food into new sellable products in-store. Distinct from just-in-time fresh production (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Use sharper demand forecasting and tighter ordering to bring less perishable stock in at each store in the first place.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever reduces INFLOW via better forecasting/ordering quantity. Distinct from changing which SKUs are stocked (r7) and from rotation discipline (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Divert genuinely unsellable food to composting, animal feed, or anaerobic digestion instead of landfill.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism captures value/diverts the truly INEDIBLE waste stream (compost, feed, biogas). Distinct from upcycling edible food into products (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Stop stocking or reduce facings of the SKUs that structurally generate the most waste relative to sales.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever changes WHAT/breadth is stocked, pruning waste-prone lines. Distinct from forecasting volume of a given line (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Extend the usable window with better packaging, coatings, ripening control, or tighter cold-chain handling.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism lengthens product LIFESPAN through technology/handling. Counts modified-atmosphere packaging, coatings, temperature control. Distinct from clearing stock faster (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Accept and merchandise cosmetically imperfect produce as a 'wonky' value line so it is sold rather than rejected.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever creates DEMAND acceptance for cosmetically substandard stock (wonky/ugly produce ranges). Distinct from price clearance of near-expiry items (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Bake and prepare deli and bakery items in smaller, more frequent batches timed to demand rather than stocking up early.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism schedules in-store PRODUCTION to demand to avoid overproduction of made-on-site goods. Distinct from raw-goods ordering (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Set store-level waste KPIs with targets, dashboards, and incentives so managers and staff actively minimise shrink.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is a BEHAVIOURAL/management mechanism: metrics, targets, incentives driving waste-reducing behaviour. Distinct from any physical or pricing tactic.

reference_answers.idea: Let employees buy or take near-expiry stock at heavy discount, providing a reliable internal outlet before disposal.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism uses an INTERNAL (staff/employee) consumption channel to absorb surplus. Distinct from external resale (r3) and donation (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Renegotiate supplier terms toward smaller, more frequent deliveries, consignment, or supplier takeback to shift spoilage risk upstream.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is UPSTREAM CONTRACTING/logistics with suppliers (delivery frequency, consignment, risk transfer). Distinct from internal DC redistribution (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Move surplus stock between stores via the distribution centres so slow-selling locations don't waste what fast ones could sell.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism rebalances inventory ACROSS the chain's own network/DCs. Distinct from supplier-side contracting (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Use loyalty rewards, recipe prompts, or bundles to steer shoppers toward soon-to-expire stock without cutting the shelf price.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever stimulates demand for at-risk stock through NON-PRICE promotion (loyalty points, recipes, bundles, nudges). Distinct from markdown pricing (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Improve first-expiry-first-out rotation with expiry-tracking sensors or AI shelf monitoring so stock is sold in the right order before it lapses.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism improves inventory ROTATION/visibility (FEFO discipline, expiry tracking, sensors, AI cameras). Distinct from reducing order volume (r5).

cases.title: Gym chain loses most January joiners by March
cases.context: A budget, no-contract gym chain in the United Kingdom on a low monthly price point with lean staffing, signing a large January intake on month-to-month memberships that members can cancel at any time, with rising acquisition costs.
cases.prompt: A budget, no-contract UK gym chain signs up large numbers of new members every January, but about 60% stop turning up within eight weeks and cancel within six months. Historically the chain profited from inactive members who kept paying, but that cushion is eroding as people cancel faster and the cost of acquiring each new member keeps climbing. Memberships are month-to-month and cancellable at any time, and the low price point leaves little room to raise fees or add costly staffing. Leadership has concluded that long-term retention, not raw sign-up volume, is now the real growth lever. The surge of new joiners each January also strains the gyms and tests the patience of committed regulars. Propose distinct approaches to convert this churn-prone intake into members who stay.

reference_answers.idea: Get new members through a guided first workout fast with free intro sessions or orientation so they form an early habit.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is ONBOARDING/activation in the first sessions to convert sign-up into habit. Distinct from ongoing goal tracking (r7) and relational coaching (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Reward attendance with streaks, challenges, badges, or points that make showing up feel rewarding.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism uses GAME/reward mechanics to reinforce attendance. Distinct from financial commitment incentives (r8) and tenure loyalty perks (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Create beginner-only zones, classes, or hours so newcomers aren't intimidated by experienced regulars.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever reduces INTIMIDATION by separating/protecting beginners. Distinct from general crowding management (r6) and social belonging (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Build community through buddy systems, small-group challenges, and belonging so members feel socially committed to returning.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism creates SOCIAL ties/accountability driving retention. Distinct from one-to-one staff coaching (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Detect when attendance drops and intervene with targeted re-engagement offers and check-ins before the member cancels.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is LIFECYCLE intervention triggered by lapse signals. Counts win-back, drop-off detection, re-engagement campaigns. Distinct from soft-exit save options (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Relieve the January crush with off-peak incentives, surge staffing, or temporary extra equipment so the experience stays good.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism protects EXPERIENCE by smoothing/handling peak congestion. Counts off-peak steering, capacity expansion, surge resourcing. Distinct from beginner segmentation (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Give members a personal plan and visible progress or results tracking so they see why to keep coming.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever sustains motivation via measurable GOALS/progress/results. Distinct from first-visit onboarding (r1) and gamified rewards (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Use financial nudges like refundable deposits, show-up discounts, or pledges that reward actually attending.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism is an ECONOMIC commitment device tied to attendance. Distinct from non-monetary gamification (r2) and loyalty/tenure perks (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Keep interest fresh with new classes, rotating content, or new equipment so members don't get bored and drift away.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever prevents boredom through NOVELTY/variety of offering. Distinct from value-add amenities that raise switching cost (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Make attendance routine by letting members book fixed slots with reminders that cue a regular gym habit.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism reduces friction and builds ROUTINE via scheduling/booking/reminders. Distinct from motivational goal tracking (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Have staff learn members' names and run personal check-ins so a human relationship keeps people coming back.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever is the HUMAN/relational bond with staff. Distinct from peer social accountability (r4) and automated win-back (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Extend touchpoints beyond the gym floor with an app offering home workouts, tracking, and motivational content.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism maintains engagement DIGITALLY between visits. Distinct from in-gym programming variety (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Shift marketing and plan design to attract or commit stickier members, e.g., annual plans or cohort-based onboarding, fixing churn at the front end.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever changes WHO is recruited or how they commit at sign-up to reduce downstream churn. Distinct from retaining whoever already joined.

reference_answers.idea: Reward continued membership with milestone perks, loyalty pricing, or referral benefits that make staying pay off.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism reinforces RETENTION via tenure/loyalty rewards. Distinct from attendance-contingent incentives (r8) and gamification (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Add value-raising extras like partner discounts, recovery facilities, or bundled perks that make leaving feel like a loss.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lever raises perceived VALUE/switching cost through amenities and bundles. Distinct from fresh programming to fight boredom (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Offer freeze, pause, or downgrade options at the point of cancellation so wavering members stay on the books instead of quitting outright.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanism saves churn by offering a LOWER-commitment alternative to full cancellation (freeze, pause, downgrade). Distinct from proactive lapse win-back (r5).

cases.title: Cold-Chain Storage Marketplace With No Bookings
cases.context: An asset-light B2B platform operating across three Indian industrial clusters (Pune, Ahmedabad, Coimbatore) that aims to match independent cold-storage facility operators with food-processing and pharmaceutical SMEs needing short-term temperature-controlled storage. Settlement runs over UPI and India's digital rails, and every facility is subject to FSSAI food-safety and drug-storage compliance verification.
cases.prompt: Eighteen months after launch the platform has registered several hundred facility operators and a comparable number of SME buyers, yet almost no bookings are ever completed. Operators log in, see no demand, and stop updating their availability; buyers find too few nearby usable listings to depend on and quietly revert to the phone-based brokers they have always used. The company earns money only as a commission on completed bookings, so today it has effectively zero revenue. Its capital is limited and it cannot absorb large acquisition losses for long, and by mandate it will not build or operate its own physical facilities. Compliance verification for each facility is slow and cannot be waived. Both sides repeat the same thing in interviews: they will use the platform 'once the other side is actually there.' Propose ways to get real transactions flowing.

reference_answers.idea: Diagnose which side is structurally scarcer (likely verified, available capacity) and pour early effort into saturating that side first so the easier side always finds a match.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any answer whose core move is sequencing acquisition by deliberately over-building one identified bottleneck side before the other, regardless of which side they pick.

reference_answers.idea: Give operators standalone value independent of any booking, e.g. free capacity, temperature-log, and compliance-tracking software, so they keep their listings live even before demand exists.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps here if the idea is to make one side use the product for a non-marketplace reason (a utility/tool/CRM), keeping them engaged absent transactions; not about discounts or guaranteed demand.

reference_answers.idea: Have platform staff manually broker the first transactions by hand, phoning operators to confirm space and stitching deals together so early buyers experience reliability.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the team itself fabricates liquidity through human, non-scalable matchmaking for early deals; distinct from co-opting third-party brokers (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Abandon three-cluster spread and concentrate all supply and demand into a single city until match rates within that radius cross a usefulness threshold.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any narrowing along geography/radius to manufacture local critical mass; not category narrowing (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Serve only one product category (e.g. pharma cold-chain) so listings, compliance, and trust expectations are homogeneous and a thin market still clears.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when the lever is restricting to one use-case/vertical/segment to deepen a thin market; distinct from geographic focus (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Use limited capital to discount, rebate, or pay listing/booking incentives to whichever side is harder to attract, improving its acquisition economics.
reference_answers.scope_note: Direct price incentives/subsidies/credits to pull one side onto the platform; not the platform taking inventory risk (r7) or guaranteeing outcomes (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Pre-purchase or reserve blocks of capacity from operators (taking inventory risk without owning facilities) so a buyer searching always sees guaranteed, bookable availability.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the platform itself underwrites supply availability by committing to buy/reserve inventory; distinct from simply subsidizing (r6) and from owning physical assets, which is barred.

reference_answers.idea: Sign one large recurring buyer (e.g. a national food brand) whose committed volume keeps a base of operators busy and worth showing up for.
reference_answers.scope_note: A single big committed demand source that alone justifies supply staying active; distinct from pooling many small buyers (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Secure exclusive-listing agreements with the best operators so their available capacity appears only on the platform, concentrating fragmented supply.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps to exclusivity, must-list, or rights deals that aggregate otherwise scattered supply; not generic incentives (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Integrate booking directly into the ERP, logistics, or order systems SMEs already run, so storage gets reserved inside an existing process rather than on a destination site.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when liquidity is created by embedding the transaction into an incumbent system/workflow/API the user already lives in.

reference_answers.idea: Add verification badges, ratings, standardized SLAs, and payment escrow so a buyer dares to commit to an unknown operator for the first time.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mechanisms that remove counterparty-trust friction via reputation/verification/escrow; distinct from guaranteeing the transaction financially (r14) and from reducing booking steps (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Collect and pool many SMEs' committed storage needs ahead of time, then go source/guarantee supply against that aggregated demand (reverse-marketplace).
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when many buyers' demand is batched/pre-committed first and supply is sourced to fill it; distinct from a single anchor buyer (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Recruit the existing phone brokers and cold-chain industry associations who already aggregate both sides, onboarding their books rather than fighting them.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when the lever is enlisting pre-existing offline aggregators/communities/associations to import liquidity; distinct from in-house concierge (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Offer a money-back/spoilage guarantee or insurance on the first bookings so a buyer's downside on a bad match is capped, lowering the bar to transact.
reference_answers.scope_note: Platform absorbs outcome risk via guarantees/insurance/SLA penalties; distinct from trust signals (r11) and from capacity backstop (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Replace negotiated quotes with standardized units, transparent fixed pricing, and one-tap instant confirmation to slash the activation energy of a first transaction.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the move reduces friction/time-to-first-transaction via standardization, instant confirmation, or simplified contracting; not trust/escrow (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Target only time-critical overflow demand (a sudden surplus or a chiller breakdown) where brokers are too slow, so the platform wins precisely where alternatives fail.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when the wedge is an acute, time-sensitive, or emergency need segment that incumbents serve poorly; distinct from general vertical narrowing (r5).

cases.title: Usage-Based Developer API That Can't Convert Its Free Tier
cases.context: A US-based, bottoms-up developer-tools company offers a cloud API that turns messy documents into structured data; individual developers adopt it self-serve with no procurement, and a very generous free tier has driven adoption to hundreds of thousands of accounts. Infrastructure cost scales roughly linearly with API calls, and free usage accounts for the large majority of total traffic.
cases.prompt: The product has strong organic growth and is widely embedded in real applications, but paid conversion sits well under one percent and revenue badly lags usage. Worse, the cost of serving free traffic climbs every month and is dragging gross margin toward zero. The free tier is the company's primary distribution channel and direct competitors also give access away, so removing it outright is not an option. Adoption is entirely bottoms-up and self-serve, with no enterprise sales motion in place today, and the current single flat paid plan converts almost nobody. Leadership will not take steps that would visibly betray developer goodwill or ignite a community backlash. Propose ways to turn this large, sticky usage base into durable revenue while keeping the free-driven growth engine intact.

reference_answers.idea: Meter the core value metric (documents/calls processed) and charge pay-as-you-go above a free allowance, so the heaviest users pay in proportion to consumption.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the lever is linear/consumption pricing on the PRIMARY value unit beyond a free quota; distinct from gating secondary axes (r3) and from changing which unit is billed (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Reserve advanced capabilities (higher accuracy models, batch mode, custom schemas, webhooks) for paid plans while basic extraction stays free.
reference_answers.scope_note: Capability/good-better-best gating of product features; distinct from governance/security gating aimed at orgs (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Monetize cheap-to-promise, expensive-to-serve dimensions such as data retention window, request rate, concurrency, or history depth, charging to lift those ceilings.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when paid unlocks non-primary consumption axes (retention/throughput/concurrency/SLA-of-speed); distinct from metering the core value metric (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Keep solo developer use free but charge when usage becomes a team or organization (shared projects, member management, role assignment).
reference_answers.scope_note: Monetizes multi-user/team collaboration or seats; distinct from enterprise security/governance (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Package SSO, audit logs, RBAC, contractual SLAs, and compliance certifications as paid capabilities that IT and security buyers require.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts for admin/security/compliance/SLA gating targeted at organizational buyers; distinct from general feature tiering (r2) and seats (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Stop billing on raw API calls and reprice onto a unit that tracks value captured (e.g. successfully extracted fields, or outcomes), aligning price with worth.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when the central move is changing WHICH unit is charged for to better correlate with value; not merely metering an existing unit (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Offer prepaid credit packs and committed-use/annual discounts that pull revenue forward and convert sporadic spenders into committed accounts.
reference_answers.scope_note: Prepay, credits, commit-for-discount, or reserved-capacity pricing structures; distinct from the base-fee-plus-overage structure (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Introduce a recurring platform/subscription fee that includes a usage bucket, with metered overage beyond it (two-part tariff).
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts for two-part pricing combining a fixed recurring fee with usage overage; distinct from pure pay-as-you-go (r1) and from prepaid credits (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Lower the free allowance to a genuine hobbyist/evaluation envelope (grandfathering existing users) so commercial-scale use must move to a paid plan.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when the lever is shrinking the generosity of the free threshold itself; distinct from adding new gated axes (r3) or charging on consumption (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Use usage signals to flag high-consumption free accounts inside real companies and add a light sales-assist motion to convert and expand them.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the move adds a sales/CS-assisted expansion motion triggered by product-usage signals; distinct from any pricing-structure change.

reference_answers.idea: Keep usage free for non-commercial, personal, or below-a-revenue-threshold use, and require a paid commercial license above it (open-core/source-available style).
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps to license-terms-based monetization keyed to commercial use or company size/revenue; distinct from metered or tiered pricing of the hosted service.

reference_answers.idea: Open-source or free the core engine for self-hosting while charging for the fully managed, hosted, scaled cloud offering that most users prefer.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when free self-host is traded against a paid managed/hosted version (open-core hosting); distinct from commercial-use licensing of the same hosted product (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Build a marketplace of third-party connectors, templates, and integrations and take a cut, monetizing the ecosystem rather than only the core API.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when revenue comes from a take-rate on add-ons/partners/marketplace; distinct from charging end-users for core usage.

reference_answers.idea: Repair unit economics on free traffic via aggressive caching, cheaper inference tiers, deduplication, or bring-your-own-compute so the free base stops bleeding margin.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when the lever attacks COGS/cost-to-serve of free users rather than extracting revenue; the only margin-side reference.

reference_answers.idea: Earn revenue from parties OTHER than the developer without compromising user trust, e.g. an opt-in paid marketplace for third-party add-ons/integrations, or charging commercial downstream consumers of the public outputs — never selling user data.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps when revenue comes from a third party via transparent, opt-in, goodwill-preserving means (add-on marketplace, certified-partner fees, downstream commercial licensing); explicitly EXCLUDES selling user/usage data or covert ads, which violate the case constraint. Distinct from any user-pays mechanism.

reference_answers.idea: Use the free API as the wedge to cross-sell a separate paid product (e.g. a workflow/automation or storage layer) where willingness-to-pay is higher.
reference_answers.scope_note: Counts when monetization shifts to an adjacent/complementary product bundled or cross-sold off the free base; not repricing the core API itself.

cases.title: The Micro-Insurance Nobody Believes Will Pay
cases.context: A licensed digital micro-insurance underwriter in Nigeria sells low-premium health and crop cover through a smartphone app and USSD shortcodes, collecting premiums via mobile money and bank transfer amid persistent double-digit naira inflation. Its target market is informal-sector workers and smallholder farmers, most of whom have only ever encountered insurance as something that takes premiums and refuses to pay claims.
cases.prompt: The firm is supervised by the national insurance regulator and writes genuinely valid, actuarially sound cover, yet paying take-up is near zero and the few who buy lapse within weeks. Prospective customers assume any claim will be denied, that the company will vanish with their money, or that the offer is an outright scam, and a single rumored denial travels fast through tight informal networks. The regulatory capital rules, the licensed product structure, and the underlying pricing cannot be changed, and you cannot give the cover away free or run a sustained loss. Premiums are tiny, so per-customer acquisition and servicing budgets are minimal, and a large share of users transact on basic phones over USSD with patchy connectivity and no reliable email. The naira steadily loses real value, making customers reluctant to part with cash today for an intangible future promise. The brand has no incumbent reputation to lean on. Your goal is to grow the base of paying, retained policyholders.

reference_answers.idea: Settle claims automatically and near-instantly via parametric/index triggers (e.g., rainfall-indexed crop payout, hospital cash on admission) so the payment is observed fast.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps any idea whose core is making the payout fast, automatic, or objectively trigger-based to prove the product actually pays; includes parametric, index-based, and instant-disbursement designs. Distinct from process transparency (r11) and one-off demo payouts (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Publicize real settled claims through testimonials, local 'we paid X' events, and visible payout tallies to create social evidence the firm honors claims.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps marketing/communicating ACTUAL past payouts as proof, including community payout ceremonies and claimant testimonials. Distinct from incentivized referral (r15) and from the speed of payment itself (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Sell and service through cooperatives, faith institutions, savings groups, and respected local agents who already hold the customer's trust.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps routing distribution/servicing through pre-trusted community institutions or individuals. Distinct from corporate co-branding (r14) and from agents bearing personal liability (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Hold reserves and premiums in visible third-party escrow or a ring-fenced trust so customers can see the firm cannot abscond with their money.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps any 'funds are safely held elsewhere and can't disappear' structure: escrow, trust, segregated bank custody. Distinct from leveraging regulatory status (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Prominently surface the license, supervisory oversight, and any industry guarantee or policyholder-compensation fund as a credibility signal.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps using official regulatory status/oversight or an industry guarantee fund to signal credibility. Distinct from a privately arranged custodial escrow (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Underwrite whole groups as a mutual or takaful in which members share the risk pool and collectively police claims.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps collective ownership/mutualization, takaful, or community-pool models where the group holds a shared stake. Distinct from merely distributing via a group (r3) and from individual referral (r15).

reference_answers.idea: Offer no-claim premium refunds, cash-back, or a no-claim bonus so customers don't feel they 'wasted' money when nothing goes wrong.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps any return-of-premium, money-back, or no-claim-bonus mechanic that reduces perceived loss. Distinct from flexible pay-as-you-go pricing (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Bundle the cover into a purchase the customer already makes — seed and fertilizer, a microloan, a clinic visit, or airtime top-up.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps attaching insurance to an existing transaction or point of risk so it is acquired passively. Distinct from third-party brand co-marketing (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Denominate the benefit in real units (bags of grain, a fixed goods basket, hard-currency or gold link) so the payout holds value despite the naira.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps protecting the benefit's real value against currency erosion via indexing or in-kind settlement. Distinct from premium-return mechanics (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Let customers pay tiny, flexible, frequent premiums (daily/weekly top-ups) instead of an upfront lump sum.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps micro, flexible, or installment premium collection that lowers the commitment hurdle. Distinct from returning premiums later (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Publish simple, objective claim criteria and live claim-status tracking with no hidden fine print.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps making the claims PROCESS clear, simple, and predictable upfront. Distinct from the speed/automation of payout (r1) and from an external complaints body (r17).

reference_answers.idea: Let prospects experience a real small payout or a free-look first claim before committing, so they prove the product to themselves before buying.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps any try-before-trust, sample-payout, or guaranteed-first-claim mechanic experienced by the prospect personally. Distinct from publicizing others' claims (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Make local agents personally accountable — they vouch, co-sign, or carry reputational and financial stake in claims being honored.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps aligning agent incentives or liability with honest fulfillment. Distinct from simply using trusted agents to sell (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Borrow credibility by co-branding or partnering with a trusted telco, bank, or well-known consumer brand.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps trust transfer from an established corporate partner's brand. Distinct from community-institution distribution (r3) and from embedding in a transaction (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Reward existing policyholders for referring others, deliberately harnessing tight informal social networks.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps incentivized peer referral and word-of-mouth mechanics. Distinct from publicizing payouts as proof (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Teach how pooled insurance actually works to correct the 'they just take your money' misconception.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps educational or explanatory campaigns that build product understanding. Distinct from proof-of-payment tactics (r1, r2, r12).

reference_answers.idea: Provide a fast, independent grievance or ombudsman channel so a denied claim has visible, credible recourse.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps external or independent complaint resolution that reduces fear of arbitrary denial. Distinct from internal claims-process transparency (r11).

cases.title: The E-Gov App Citizens Won't Open
cases.context: A national government in a mid-sized European country has launched a free unified app and web portal consolidating dozens of public services — tax, benefits, permits, health appointments, vehicle registration — to move citizens off in-person offices and paper forms. The service is mandatory for the state to offer but voluntary for citizens to use, statutory identity-assurance and data-protection rules are strict, and physical offices and call centres remain fully open.
cases.prompt: Years after launch the app is barely used; most citizens still queue at counters, post paper forms, or phone helplines for tasks the app completes in minutes, and the parallel channels are expensive to keep running. Of those who do download it, many stall at the first account or identity-verification step and never come back. The service is genuinely free and works well, yet a large share of the population — older residents, rural users, non-native speakers, and the digitally hesitant — never hears about it, assumes it is not meant for them, or gives up at the first obstacle. You cannot make digital use compulsory, cannot shut the in-person and phone channels by decree, and cannot weaken the statutory identity-assurance or data-protection requirements. Budgets are fixed and there is no funding for sustained national paid advertising. Your goal is to move a durable majority of routine transactions onto the digital channel.

reference_answers.idea: Cut steps, dead-ends, and complexity in the registration and verification flow so first-time users actually get through.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps reducing or streamlining the signup/verification funnel itself. Distinct from reusing an existing credential (r2) and from letting users act before full verification (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Let citizens authenticate once with an existing national eID or bank-ID and reuse that credential across every service.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps single sign-on, reusable credentials, or federated identity that removes a separate signup. Distinct from generally simplifying the signup screens (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Deploy human helpers — libraries, post offices, community organizations, NGOs, and family — to register and coach hesitant users.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps third-party or community assisted digital enrollment. Distinct from diverting users at a government service counter (r4) and from default auto-provisioning (r5).

reference_answers.idea: At counters and call centres, actively convert the in-person or phone transaction into an app transaction during that same contact.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps channel-shifting at the moment of an existing in-person/phone interaction. Distinct from community-helper enrollment (r3) and from giving digital faster turnaround (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Auto-create accounts for citizens on an opt-out basis so the account already exists when they need it.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps pre-provisioned or default-created accounts. Distinct from life-event-triggered outreach (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Reach citizens with the relevant service exactly at a triggering life event — birth, moving home, job loss, licence renewal.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps event-timed outreach that creates a concrete reason to adopt now. Distinct from recurring habit notifications (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Recruit trusted local figures — doctors, faith leaders, community groups — to endorse and promote the service.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps credibility-borrowing endorsements by trusted intermediaries. Distinct from peer-to-peer referral (r8) and from staffed enrollment help (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Enable and prompt users to onboard relatives and neighbours through manage-on-behalf, sharing, and invites.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps peer-to-peer and word-of-mouth diffusion mechanics among ordinary users. Distinct from authority/messenger endorsement (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Make the app DO things for citizens — pre-filled forms, automatic reminders, proactively surfaced entitlements — so it pulls demand.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps shifting from a passive form-portal to a proactive service that delivers value unasked. Distinct from merely communicating convenience (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Build low-literacy, multilingual, voice, low-bandwidth, and accessibility-compliant versions for currently excluded groups.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps redesigning the product for accessibility and inclusion of older, rural, non-native, or disabled users. Distinct from human assistance (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Give people repeated reasons and reminders to open the app via notifications, status updates, and recurring tasks.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps habit-forming, retention, and re-engagement nudges. Distinct from one-time life-event triggers (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Make the time and effort saving vivid at the point of choice, e.g., showing the queue/wait versus minutes in-app.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps communication and framing that surfaces the convenience advantage. Distinct from actually giving digital faster service (r16) and from building proactive features (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Expose the services via APIs inside apps citizens already use — banks, private platforms — rather than forcing them into a new government app.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps meeting users in existing private channels through an open/embedded ecosystem. Distinct from redirecting at a government counter (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Let users do something useful immediately at a low assurance tier, stepping up identity checks only when a task requires it.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps tiered or progressive trust so value precedes full ID verification. Distinct from generally simplifying signup (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Concentrate first on the most receptive segments or regions to build a visible critical mass and social norm before broadening.
