reference_answers.scope_note: Maps targeted early-adopter, segment, or geographic seeding to create diffusion momentum. Distinct from individual referral mechanics (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Give the digital channel materially better service — faster turnaround, priority handling, or features unavailable on paper.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps making digital objectively preferable through a service-level advantage. Distinct from merely framing convenience (r12) and from counter redirection (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Address data-protection and surveillance fears with clear, trustworthy data-handling communication and user controls.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maps overcoming privacy or trust hesitancy about government data use. Distinct from general accessibility design (r10).

cases.title: Maxed-Out Injection Molding Plant
cases.context: A contract injection-molding manufacturer near Puebla, Mexico runs twelve presses across two shifts, producing OEM-approved plastic interior trim under fixed multi-year supply contracts with three automakers. The plant is selling 100% of what it can physically produce while qualified new orders keep arriving.
cases.prompt: The committed order book already exceeds what the plant can ship by roughly 25%, and that gap is widening as the automaker customers ramp their own production. For the next eighteen months the parent group has frozen capital expenditure, the building is at its footprint limit with no room to extend the floor, and the OEM-approved part geometries and material grades cannot be altered without a full requalification the customers refuse to fund. The collective labor agreement caps total headcount and fixes shift premiums, so simply hiring a third crew at premium rates is off the table. On-time delivery and the contractual quality/PPAP requirements must be held; missing either triggers penalties that erase any throughput gain. Leadership wants to ship materially more sellable product out of the existing asset base inside these limits. Where should they look, and what concretely should they do?

reference_answers.idea: Identify the single gating process stage and schedule the entire plant to keep it continuously fed and never idle, sequencing all other operations around it.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any answer about locating the limiting stage and subordinating/finite-scheduling the rest of the plant to it (drum-buffer-rope, throughput-accounting prioritization at the constraint); not the specific loss-reduction levers below.

reference_answers.idea: Cut mold/die changeover time with quick-die-exchange, externalized setup, and standardized tooling so the constraint spends more time running.
reference_answers.scope_note: SMED, single-minute die exchange, setup standardization, and grouping like-tooling jobs to reduce the number/duration of changeovers.

reference_answers.idea: Raise availability of the constraint presses through preventive/predictive maintenance and faster breakdown response to eliminate unplanned downtime.
reference_answers.scope_note: Maintenance programs, condition monitoring, critical-spares strategy, and MTTR reduction focused on the bottleneck; availability losses, not setup or cycle speed.

reference_answers.idea: Run the constraint faster within spec by optimizing process parameters such as cooling time and injection profile via designed experiments.
reference_answers.scope_note: Speeding the machine cycle, parameter optimization/DOE, and eliminating micro-stops or slow-cycle losses (OEE performance), staying inside approved specs.

reference_answers.idea: Eliminate scrap and rework at and upstream of the constraint so its hours are never consumed making defective parts.
reference_answers.scope_note: First-pass yield, in-line inspection that stops bad parts before the bottleneck, and cutting rework loops; quality losses specifically as wasted constraint time.

reference_answers.idea: Increase good parts produced per machine shot via higher-cavitation or redesigned molds that fit existing presses.
reference_answers.scope_note: More parts per cycle through tooling/mold cavitation or yield-per-shot redesign, distinct from running the existing tool faster (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Move eligible parts onto underutilized non-bottleneck presses or alternate internal process routes to balance load away from the constraint.
reference_answers.scope_note: Load-balancing across machines, qualifying a second press for a part, routing whole SKUs off the constraint; internal capacity only.

reference_answers.idea: Subcontract peak or low-value volume to external molders to free internal constraint capacity for the most valuable work.
reference_answers.scope_note: External/toll molding of overflow, make-vs-buy for specific SKUs, using outside capacity to absorb demand.

reference_answers.idea: Allocate scarce constraint hours to products with the highest throughput per constraint-hour and reprice or shed the worst offenders.
reference_answers.scope_note: Throughput-per-bottleneck-hour ranking, product-mix optimization, dropping/renegotiating low-value SKUs; demand-allocation, not capacity creation.

reference_answers.idea: Hold a protective WIP/time buffer immediately before the constraint so upstream variability can never starve it.
reference_answers.scope_note: Strategic decoupling inventory or time buffer positioned ahead of the bottleneck to protect throughput; not finished-goods or demand buffering.

reference_answers.idea: Keep the constraint running through breaks, lunches, and shift handovers using relief operators or staggered breaks within the existing crew.
reference_answers.scope_note: Hot-seat changeovers, break/lunch relief, eliminating handover idle on the constraint by reallocating current labor within the headcount cap.

reference_answers.idea: Automate load/unload, ejection, and material feed at the constraint to remove operator-paced delays each cycle.
reference_answers.scope_note: Robots, auto-eject, conveyors, or feed automation at the bottleneck that shortens non-value handling time per cycle without adding headcount.

reference_answers.idea: Flatten order arrival against fixed capacity using scheduling agreements, build-ahead, and lead-time commitments with customers.
reference_answers.scope_note: Order leveling, available-to-promise discipline, collaborative forecasting and build-ahead to smooth peaks; managing inflow rather than output.

reference_answers.idea: Make a low-cost retrofit to the existing constraint press—added cooling, an accumulator, faster auxiliaries—that lifts its rate without a new line.
reference_answers.scope_note: Minor physical upgrades raising the existing machine's ceiling; distinct from buying a press (barred), parameter tuning (r4), and tooling changes (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Run the constraint lights-out during nights or weekends with remote monitoring to add operating hours without added labor.
reference_answers.scope_note: Extending the constraint's running hours via automation/remote monitoring rather than hiring or paying shift premiums; distinct from in-shift relief (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Move sub-operations currently done at the constraint (trimming, inserts, finishing) onto adjacent non-constraint stations so the bottleneck does less per part.
reference_answers.scope_note: Rebalancing work content off the constraint onto other stations; distinct from rerouting whole parts elsewhere (r7).

cases.title: Failed Last-Mile Deliveries at Scale
cases.context: A third-party last-mile parcel carrier in Bengaluru, India delivers more than 80,000 e-commerce parcels a day across dense residential blocks and gated communities, a large share cash-on-delivery, using a high-churn gig fleet of two-wheeler riders. It is paid a fixed fee per delivered parcel by its e-commerce clients.
cases.prompt: A large fraction of stops fail on the first attempt and must be re-attempted one or more times, inflating cost per parcel, blowing through delivery-promise dates, and driving complaints from both merchants and end customers. The per-parcel rates paid by the e-commerce clients are locked for the year and cannot be renegotiated, and the carrier cannot require those merchants to change anything in their own checkout or app flows. The rider fleet is gig-based and churns fast, the vehicle type is fixed to two-wheelers by the city's roads and parking, and warehouse and sortation capacity are fixed. The published delivery-promise dates shown to customers cannot be loosened. Management wants to sharply reduce the volume and cost of failed and repeated delivery attempts inside all of these constraints. What concrete moves are available to them?

reference_answers.idea: Deliver within a recipient-chosen or predicted time window so someone is present to receive.
reference_answers.scope_note: Slotted/appointment delivery, narrow windows, or timing attempts to recipient availability; the demand-side commitment, distinct from the routing engine that honors it (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Send a live ETA and arrival alert ahead of the stop so the recipient is ready when the rider reaches the door.
reference_answers.scope_note: SMS/app ETA, 'rider arriving' alerts, and readiness confirmation sent before the rider arrives; not the at-door call (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Validate, correct, and precisely geocode addresses before dispatch to cut not-found and wrong-location failures.
reference_answers.scope_note: Address verification, GPS-pin capture, landmark/locality correction, and deduping bad addresses upstream of the rider.

reference_answers.idea: Divert hard-to-reach parcels to lockers, partner kirana stores, or pickup points instead of attempting the home.
reference_answers.scope_note: PUDO networks, parcel lockers, neighborhood-store pickup as fallback or opt-in default; out-of-home receipt.

reference_answers.idea: Leave the parcel in a recipient-authorized safe location with photo proof, with no face-to-face handoff required.
reference_answers.scope_note: Leave-at-door / authority-to-leave with proof-of-delivery photo for non-COD parcels; unattended drop the recipient pre-authorized.

reference_answers.idea: Hand the parcel to a neighbor, security guard, building reception, or other designated alternate recipient.
reference_answers.scope_note: Gatekeeper/guard handoff, neighbor delivery, building-desk receipt, or a named alternate accepting on the recipient's behalf.

reference_answers.idea: Convert cash-on-delivery to digital prepay or at-door QR/UPI payment to remove cash-not-ready and exact-change failures.
reference_answers.scope_note: Carrier-side prepay nudges, doorstep QR/UPI, and reducing COD share to eliminate payment-driven failed attempts.

reference_answers.idea: Let recipients reschedule, redirect, or hold a parcel before the attempt via a link, avoiding a wasted trip entirely.
reference_answers.scope_note: Pre-attempt customer control to change date/address/location or hold at hub; preventing the doomed trip before it happens.

reference_answers.idea: Sequence routes using availability and preference signals so stops are visited when receipt is most likely, e.g. working households in the evening.
reference_answers.scope_note: Route optimization conditioned on time-windows/availability and clustering by readiness; the supply-side sequencing engine, distinct from offering windows (r1).

reference_answers.idea: Pay and score riders on successful first-attempt delivery rather than raw attempt count to curb premature 'not home' marks.
reference_answers.scope_note: Incentive/penalty restructuring, success-rate scoring, and accountability for false fails; aligning gig-rider effort.

reference_answers.idea: Score stops for failure risk from history, geography, and COD status, then apply pre-emptive interventions only where needed.
reference_answers.scope_note: ML/heuristic risk targeting that triggers a call, prepay push, or relocation selectively on high-risk parcels; the targeting layer, not a fix itself.

reference_answers.idea: Solve physical entry to gated societies and apartments via gate codes, RWA/society agreements, and pre-cleared guard entry lists.
reference_answers.scope_note: Access credentials, building/society partnerships, intercom or guard pre-authorization to get the rider inside.

reference_answers.idea: Require the rider to call or message the recipient on arrival before any stop can be marked failed.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mandatory two-way contact at the door to prevent premature/fake failures; the contact happens at arrival, distinct from pre-trip ETA (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Bundle multiple parcels for the same recipient, household, or building into a single trip and handoff.
reference_answers.scope_note: Batching shipments to one address or one building per visit to cut repeat trips; consolidation of deliveries.

reference_answers.idea: Collect delivery instructions, alternate phone numbers, and drop preferences directly from recipients via the carrier's own first contact, independent of merchant checkout.
reference_answers.scope_note: Enriching delivery data post-handoff through the carrier's channel/app since merchant checkout is fixed; building reusable recipient preference profiles.

reference_answers.idea: When a first attempt fails, triage and batch the retry intelligently—next-day clustering, switch to a pickup point, or hold at hub—rather than blindly re-running the same route.
reference_answers.scope_note: Making the unavoidable retries cheaper via retry routing and channel-switching; distinct from preventing the first failure.

cases.title: The One-and-Done Donor
cases.context: A mid-sized UK-registered animal-welfare charity raising about GBP 4M a year, regulated by the Fundraising Regulator and the Charity Commission, with most income arriving as individual cash gifts under GBP 50 from emergency appeals and online advertising, and Gift Aid reclaimed via HMRC.
cases.prompt: The charity recruits tens of thousands of first-time donors every year, but the overwhelming majority never give a second time: second-year retention of newly acquired cash donors sits in the low teens of a percent, far below its long-standing committed-giving cohorts. The fully loaded cost of acquiring a new donor has climbed and now exceeds the value of the typical single gift, so on a unit basis the charity loses money on each new supporter unless that person gives again. It cannot increase total fundraising spend or add fundraising and marketing headcount, and it cannot lift the average ask amount without measurably depressing response rates. It must operate inside Fundraising Regulator rules that cap contact frequency and require explicit, channel-specific consent, and inside UK GDPR, which forbids buying third-party contact lists and mandates opt-in for email and SMS. The board will not approve anything that jeopardises the charity's accreditation or public-trust standing, and brand rules forbid any messaging that misrepresents how money is used. Propose the broadest possible set of structurally distinct approaches to fix these economics.

reference_answers.idea: Build a structured early-relationship sequence triggered by the first gift that orients and nurtures the donor before any further ask is made.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any planned multi-touch welcome/nurture programme for new donors (welcome series, first-90-days journey, relationship-building before second solicitation); not a single thank-you (r5) and not purely a fast resolicitation (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Convert one-off cash givers into regular committed donors via standing order or Direct Debit so giving becomes automatic rather than re-decided.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any move to shift payment cadence to recurring/monthly/regular-giving or membership-dues style commitments; about changing the giving frequency mechanism, distinct from making a single repeat ask (r3) or easier checkout (r10).

reference_answers.idea: Solicit a prompt second gift while the donor is still emotionally engaged, treating speed and timing of the next ask as the lever.
reference_answers.scope_note: Timing-driven resolicitation (second-gift-within-X-days, strike-while-warm, fast follow-up appeal); about WHEN the next ask lands, not the onboarding content (r1) or converting to recurring (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Close the loop by showing donors the concrete outcome their specific gift produced, so the giving feels consequential.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reporting back results/outcomes/impact of the gift (this is what your donation did); distinct from saying thank-you (r5) and from matching messaging to the trigger cause (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Deliver a genuine, personal acknowledgement such as a thank-you call or handwritten note that raises the donor's felt appreciation.
reference_answers.scope_note: Acknowledgement/thanking mechanisms (welcome calls, handwritten cards, gratitude messaging); about feeling valued, not about showing outcomes (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Allocate higher-touch handling to donors predicted to be higher value or higher affinity rather than treating all new donors identically.
reference_answers.scope_note: Value/affinity-based segmentation of stewardship effort (tiered treatment, concentrate effort on best prospects); the general allocation lever, distinct from upgrade ladders (r11) and lapse-risk triggers (r17).

reference_answers.idea: Reweight acquisition toward sources and channels that historically yield better-retaining donors even at equal cost-per-acquisition.
reference_answers.scope_note: Changing the donor intake mix to improve downstream retention (recruit through higher-LTV channels, drop poorly-retaining sources); a front-end acquisition-quality lever, not anything done after the gift.

reference_answers.idea: Tailor subsequent communications to the specific cause or appeal that triggered the donor's first gift.
reference_answers.scope_note: Aligning future content to the donor's original motivation/cause/appeal (rescue donors hear rescue stories); about relevance/personalisation of cause, distinct from generic impact reporting (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Reframe the relationship around belonging and membership so the donor adopts a supporter identity rather than a transactional stance.
reference_answers.scope_note: Building belonging/identity/community (supporter clubs, named membership, insider status, us-language); about affiliation, not payment cadence (r2) or a tangible product (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Make repeat giving effortless via stored payment details, prefilled forms, and one-tap renewal.
reference_answers.scope_note: Lowering the mechanical effort of giving again (saved cards, one-click, wallet pay, prefilled donor data); about ease of transaction, not its timing (r3) or cadence (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Cultivate donors who show greater capacity up a giving ladder toward larger or mid-value gifts.
reference_answers.scope_note: Deliberate upgrading/development of higher-capacity donors (mid-value programme, ask-string escalation, major-donor pipeline); about growing gift size over time, distinct from broad stewardship allocation (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Run a targeted win-back programme for donors who have already gone quiet, with reactivation-specific appeals.
reference_answers.scope_note: Re-engaging already-lapsed/dormant donors (we miss you, reactivation appeals, win-back); operates after lapse, distinct from preventing lapse via risk triggers (r17).

reference_answers.idea: Anchor repeat gifts to recurring personal or calendar occasions such as birthdays, anniversaries, or in-memoriam tributes.
reference_answers.scope_note: Tribute/occasion/memorial giving that creates natural renewal moments; about hooking giving to dated occasions, distinct from a saleable sponsorship product (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Maximise Gift Aid declarations on retained gifts to raise each donor's effective value without raising the ask amount.
reference_answers.scope_note: Improving unit economics through HMRC Gift Aid uptake/declaration capture (claim the 25% uplift); a value-per-donor lever that respects the no-higher-ask constraint, not a retention-behaviour lever.

reference_answers.idea: Plant long-horizon intent such as gifts in wills to extract lifetime value from loyal supporters.
reference_answers.scope_note: Legacy/bequest/gift-in-will or long-term pledge cultivation; about future-dated lifetime value, distinct from near-term recurring conversion (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Offer a tangible giving proposition such as sponsoring a named animal that has built-in renewal logic.
reference_answers.scope_note: Designing a giving product/proposition with intrinsic recurrence (adopt-an-animal, sponsorship packs, subscription gift); the product-design lever, distinct from simply switching payment to recurring (r2) or building identity (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Score donors for lapse risk and fire targeted interventions before they actually drop off.
reference_answers.scope_note: Predictive/at-risk modelling driving pre-emptive save actions (propensity-to-lapse triggers, early-warning intervention); risk-timing targeting, distinct from value-based stewardship (r6) and post-lapse win-back (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Use matched-giving campaigns and visible peer commitment to spur the second gift through social and reciprocity pressure.
reference_answers.scope_note: Matched/challenge funding and social-proof mechanics (your gift doubled, donor counts, peer behaviour) to motivate the next gift; a social-incentive lever, distinct from rational impact reporting (r4).

cases.title: Spindles That Sit Still
cases.context: A privately owned precision-machining job shop in the US Midwest runs several debt-financed 5-axis CNC machines, supplying machined aerospace components under AS9100/ISO certification on competitively bid contracts.
cases.prompt: The shop's machines run only a single day shift and are actually cutting metal roughly 30% of available hours, sitting idle through most nights and weekends while their financing payments accrue regardless of use, so low utilization is steadily eroding margin. The work it wins comes through competitive bids, and it cannot raise prices without losing the aerospace customers that anchor its revenue. It cannot staff a full second shift of skilled machinists because the regional labor market for that skill is depleted, and it cannot take on new debt to add machines. It must preserve its AS9100/ISO certifications, full part traceability, and its on-time delivery commitments to existing customers, none of which can be compromised. Capital for major new equipment or a new facility is off the table for the foreseeable future. Propose the broadest possible set of structurally distinct approaches to lift utilization and repair the economics within these limits.

reference_answers.idea: Add pallet changers, bar feeders, or robotic part handling so machines run unattended through nights and weekends without a full crew.
reference_answers.scope_note: Any unattended/automated machine-tending that converts idle hours to cutting hours without proportional labor (lights-out, robotic load/unload, pallet pools); distinct from getting one human to tend several machines (r8).

reference_answers.idea: Reshape labor coverage with staggered, partial, or weekend shifts to extend the hours machines are attended without a full second crew.
reference_answers.scope_note: Rearranging human schedules to widen operating hours (split shifts, weekend-only crew, four-day-ten staggering, overlapping starts); about labor time-coverage, not automation (r1) or growing the labor pool (r16).

reference_answers.idea: Cut non-cutting setup time through SMED-style tooling and fixturing so each available hour yields more spindle-up time.
reference_answers.scope_note: Reducing setup/changeover/non-cutting time (SMED, quick-change fixtures, preset tooling, standardized work-holding); about machine-on-but-not-cutting losses, distinct from material readiness (r14).

reference_answers.idea: Sequence and dispatch jobs to minimize gaps and batch similar setups so machine time is densely packed.
reference_answers.scope_note: Better scheduling/sequencing/dispatching and job batching to close idle gaps (finite-capacity scheduling, similar-setup grouping); the planning lever, distinct from diagnosing root causes of idle (r9).

reference_answers.idea: Sell idle machine-time outward by subcontracting for other shops or listing capacity on a manufacturing marketplace.
reference_answers.scope_note: Monetizing spare capacity by becoming a sub/supplier to others (Xometry-style marketplaces, contract machining for peers, fill-in work); selling time outward, distinct from a reciprocal peer alliance (r11).

reference_answers.idea: Use lead-time tiers or discounts that pull customer demand into the currently idle night and weekend windows.
reference_answers.scope_note: Price/lead-time incentives that shift WHEN demand arrives into troughs (expedite premiums, off-peak discounts, flexible-date pricing); a demand-shaping lever inside existing customers, not winning new work (r13).

reference_answers.idea: Pursue a different blend of work, such as higher-volume or steadier programs, to fill the lumpy troughs in the current order book.
reference_answers.scope_note: Changing the type/mix of jobs pursued to smooth demand (target higher-volume runs, steadier repeat work, less lumpy customers); about what work is sought, distinct from new adjacent service lines (r12).

reference_answers.idea: Train operators to run multiple machines at once so a single machinist keeps several spindles cutting.
reference_answers.scope_note: Multi-machine tending via flexible/cross-trained labor (one operator, several machines, broadened skill matrix); raising machines-per-worker, distinct from robotic automation (r1) and adding shifts (r2).

reference_answers.idea: Measure OEE and trace exactly why machines sit idle, then attack the dominant root cause.
reference_answers.scope_note: Diagnostic measurement of idle causes (OEE/utilization analytics, machine monitoring, waiting-on-X root-cause analysis); the find-the-cause lever, distinct from scheduling action (r4).

reference_answers.idea: Reduce unplanned breakdown downtime through condition-based and predictive maintenance so capacity isn't lost to failures.
reference_answers.scope_note: Cutting unplanned downtime via maintenance strategy (predictive/condition-based/preventive upkeep, spindle health monitoring); recovering hours lost to failure, distinct from setup losses (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Form a reciprocal capacity-sharing alliance with peer shops to absorb each other's overflow and troughs.
reference_answers.scope_note: Mutual/co-op capacity sharing among peers (overflow swaps, shared-capacity alliance, regional shop network); reciprocal arrangement, distinct from one-directional brokering of your own time (r5).

reference_answers.idea: Monetize idle time with adjacent offerings such as rapid prototyping, low-volume runs, or inspection-as-a-service.
reference_answers.scope_note: Adding adjacent revenue services that use the equipment (prototyping, short-run, metrology/inspection services, finishing); creating new demand categories, distinct from rebalancing the existing job mix (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Win more fill work by quoting faster and bidding more jobs through automated or streamlined estimating.
reference_answers.scope_note: Raising the velocity/volume of the sales pipeline (automated quoting, faster estimating, bidding more RFQs); a demand-acquisition lever on the front office, distinct from pricing incentives (r6).

reference_answers.idea: Stage and kit raw stock, programs, and tooling so jobs are ready and machines never wait on inputs.
reference_answers.scope_note: Ensuring upstream readiness so machines don't idle waiting (kitting, material staging, pre-released NC programs, tool pre-staging); input-availability lever, distinct from setup speed itself (r3).

reference_answers.idea: Lock in a long-term take-or-pay or volume-commitment customer to fill the predictable trough with steady work.
reference_answers.scope_note: Securing committed anchor volume to fill troughs (take-or-pay, long-term supply agreement, dedicated-capacity contract); guaranteeing baseload demand, distinct from chasing a steadier mix opportunistically (r7).

reference_answers.idea: Grow the future skilled-labor supply through apprenticeships and training partnerships to eventually staff more hours.
reference_answers.scope_note: Expanding the skilled-worker pool over time (apprenticeships, trade-school pipelines, in-house training programs); a longer-horizon labor-supply lever, distinct from rescheduling existing staff (r2).
